title: ZERO exceptions! C before E, I, Y = always /s/.
shortDescription: C says /s/ before E, I, or Y.
fullExplanation: C says /s/ before E, I, or Y. Otherwise, C says /k/.

title: G TENDS to soften before E, I, Y — but has exceptions unlike C.
shortDescription: G may soften to /j/ before E, I, or Y.
fullExplanation: G may soften to /j/ before E, I, or Y. Otherwise, G always says /g/.

title: I, U, V, J never fly solo at the end — they always bring a friend.
shortDescription: English words do not end
fullExplanation: English words do not end in I, U, V, or J. A companion letter is added.

title: One vowel + end of word? S gets a buddy — double it to SS.
shortDescription: After a single vowel, a base word
fullExplanation: After a single vowel, a base word does not end in S alone. It doubles to SS.

title: Short vowel + F, L, S, or Z at the end? Double it up: FF, LL, SS, ZZ.
shortDescription: In a one-syllable word, after a
fullExplanation: In a one-syllable word, after a single short vowel, final /f/, /l/, /s/, or /z/ is doubled.

title: Short vowel needs a shield? CK is the bodyguard for /k/.
shortDescription: CK is used right after a single
fullExplanation: CK is used right after a single short vowel at the end of a one-syllable word.
yesWords: pocket (short O → CK in stressed syllable)
noWords: bake (long A → just K with E)
stoke (long O → just K with E)

title: Short vowel before /ch/? Add a T shield: TCH.
shortDescription: TCH is used right after a single
fullExplanation: TCH is used right after a single short vowel.

title: Short vowel before /j/? Add a D shield: DGE.
shortDescription: DGE is used right after a single
fullExplanation: DGE is used right after a single short vowel.

title: End of the road? AY. Middle of the word? AI.
shortDescription: AY is used at the end of a
fullExplanation: AY is used at the end of a base word for /ay/. AI is used everywhere else.

title: Same pattern as AY/AI — OY at the end, OI in the middle.
shortDescription: OY is used at the end of a
fullExplanation: OY is used at the end of a base word for /oi/. OI is used everywhere else.

title: Q is never alone — U is always its partner.
shortDescription: Q always appears with U.
fullExplanation: Q always appears with U. QU says /kw/. No English word has Q without U.

title: Vowel suffix coming? Silent E steps aside.
shortDescription: Drop the silent E when adding
fullExplanation: Drop the silent E when adding a suffix that starts with a vowel.
noWords: dyeing (keep E to distinguish from dying)
noticeable (E kept to preserve soft C)
courageous (E kept to preserve soft G)

title: Consonant suffix coming? Silent E stays put.
shortDescription: Keep the silent E when adding
fullExplanation: Keep the silent E when adding a suffix that starts with a consonant.

title: Stressed ending + 1 vowel + 1 consonant? Double up before a vowel suffix.
shortDescription: Double the final consonant before
fullExplanation: Double the final consonant before a vowel suffix when the word ends in 1 vowel + 1 consonant (stressed).
noWords: open → opening (stress NOT on last syllable)
visit → visiting (stress NOT on last syllable)
enter → entering (stress NOT on last syllable)

title: Consonant + Y? Y becomes I — unless the suffix already starts with I.
shortDescription: Consonant + Y at the end?
fullExplanation: Consonant + Y at the end? Change Y to I before any suffix (except suffixes starting with I).
noWords: baby → babyish (keep Y before -ish with I)

title: Vowel + Y = best friends. Never separate them.
shortDescription: Vowel + Y at the end?
fullExplanation: Vowel + Y at the end? Keep the Y when adding any suffix.

title: The most famous E job — it makes the vowel say its own name.
shortDescription: Silent E Job 1:
fullExplanation: Silent E Job 1: Makes the preceding vowel say its long sound.
yesWords: complete (E says /ee/ thanks to final E)
noWords: have (E protects V, not long A)
give (E protects V, not long I)
love (E protects V, not long O)
gone (E does not make O long)

title: V and U are shy — they need E to stand at the end.
shortDescription: Silent E Job 2:
fullExplanation: Silent E Job 2: English words do not end in V or U, so E follows them.

title: Without the E, C would snap to /k/ and G would snap to /g/.
shortDescription: Silent E Job 3:
fullExplanation: Silent E Job 3: Keeps C and G soft (/s/ and /j/) at the end of a word.

title: The -LE ending would be vowel-less without that silent E.
shortDescription: Silent E Job 4:
fullExplanation: Silent E Job 4: Every syllable needs a vowel. E provides one where needed.

title: Without E, 'house' would be 'hous' — looks like a weird plural!
shortDescription: Silent E Job 5:
fullExplanation: Silent E Job 5: Prevents a word from looking like a plural ending in S.
yesWords: dense (without E → dens = plural of den)

title: Three costumes for one suffix: /ed/, /d/, or /t/ — listen to the last sound.
shortDescription: The suffix -ED has 3 sounds:
fullExplanation: The suffix -ED has 3 sounds: /ed/ after T or D, /d/ after voiced, /t/ after unvoiced.

title: If the ending already hisses or buzzes, -ES adds a whole new syllable.
shortDescription: Add -ES (not just -S) when
fullExplanation: Add -ES (not just -S) when a word ends in S, X, Z, CH, or SH.

title: Consonant + Y plurals: Y steps out, I + ES step in.
shortDescription: Consonant + Y at the end?
fullExplanation: Consonant + Y at the end? Change Y to I and add -ES to make it plural.
noWords: monkey → monkeys (vowel + Y, just add S)
key → keys (vowel + Y, just add S)
day → days (vowel + Y, just add S)
toy → toys (vowel + Y, just add S)

title: Both say /shun/ — TION is far more common, SION is the specialist.
shortDescription: -TION and -SION both say /shun/.
fullExplanation: -TION and -SION both say /shun/. -TION follows consonants/long vowels. -SION follows other patterns.

title: Can the base stand alone? ABLE. Can't? IBLE.
shortDescription: -ABLE joins complete words.
fullExplanation: -ABLE joins complete words. -IBLE joins roots that can't stand alone.

title: Need an adjective? Slap on -AL and the noun transforms.
shortDescription: The suffix -AL turns a noun
fullExplanation: The suffix -AL turns a noun or verb into an adjective.

title: Adjective ends in L? Adding -LY gives you double LL. That's correct!
shortDescription: Add -LY to adjectives to make
fullExplanation: Add -LY to adjectives to make adverbs. If the adjective ends in L, the result is -LLY.

title: The I in -ING acts like a shield — it keeps C and G soft.
shortDescription: The letter I in a suffix
fullExplanation: The letter I in a suffix keeps C soft (/s/) and G soft (/j/) in the base word.

title: The shield trio: CK, DGE, TCH protect short vowels. Remove the shield for long vowels.
shortDescription: After a short vowel: use CK, DGE, TCH.
fullExplanation: After a short vowel: use CK, DGE, TCH. After anything else (long vowel, consonant, two vowels): use K, GE, CH.
yesWords: back vs. bake (short → CK, long → K)
match vs. march (short → TCH, consonant → CH)
badge vs. cage (short → DGE, long → GE)
stock vs. stoke (short → CK, long → K)

title: O before M, N, V, TH often sounds like /u/ — 'love' rhymes with 'dove', not 'stove'.
shortDescription: O says /u/ when followed by
fullExplanation: O says /u/ when followed by M, N, V, or TH.
noWords: bone (O before N but long O — silent E job)
home (O before M but long O — silent E job)
over (O before V but long O — silent E job)

title: Not every final E makes a long vowel — check Jobs 2 through 5.
shortDescription: When vowel-consonant-E doesn't
fullExplanation: When vowel-consonant-E doesn't make a long vowel, the E has a different silent E job (2–5).

title: Five silent pairs: KN, WR, GN, MB, MN — the first letter hides but stays in spelling.
shortDescription: Silent consonant families:
fullExplanation: Silent consonant families: KN (K silent), WR (W silent), GN (G silent), MB (B silent), MN (N silent).

title: In WALK and CALM, the L is a ghost — silent but never removed from spelling.
shortDescription: More silent L patterns:
fullExplanation: More silent L patterns: LK — the L is silent. LM — the L is silent.

title: W is a vowel-warper — it bends the A from /a/ toward /o/.
shortDescription: After W, the letter A often
fullExplanation: After W, the letter A often says /o/ instead of /a/.

title: L is another vowel-warper — it shifts ALL, OLL, and ULL into unique sounds.
shortDescription: L modifies certain vowels:
fullExplanation: L modifies certain vowels: ALL = /awl/, OLL = /ohl/, ULL = /ool/.

title: One of the most reliable patterns in English — IGHT always says /eye-t/.
shortDescription: The pattern -IGHT always
fullExplanation: The pattern -IGHT always says /eye-t/. GH is silent in this pattern.

title: Weird spelling? Check the passport — Greek, French, and Latin words travel with their own rules.
shortDescription: Borrowed words keep their
fullExplanation: Borrowed words keep their original spelling patterns: Greek PH, CH; French CH; Latin TI.

title: TION says /shun/
shortDescription: Words ending in TION always say /shun/: nation, action, education.
fullExplanation: Words ending in TION always say /shun/: nation, action, education.

title: SION says /shun/ or /zhun/
shortDescription: SION says /shun/ after a consonant (tension) and /zhun/ after a vowel (vision).
fullExplanation: SION says /shun/ after a consonant (tension) and /zhun/ after a vowel (vision).

title: CIAN says /shun/
shortDescription: CIAN means a person: musician, physician, electrician.
fullExplanation: CIAN means a person: musician, physician, electrician.

title: CIAL says /shul/
shortDescription: CIAL appears in adjectives: special, official, financial.
fullExplanation: CIAL appears in adjectives: special, official, financial.

title: TIAL says /shul/
shortDescription: TIAL also says /shul/: partial, essential, initial.
fullExplanation: TIAL also says /shul/: partial, essential, initial.

title: CIOUS/TIOUS says /shus/
shortDescription: Words ending in CIOUS or TIOUS: delicious, cautious, spacious.
fullExplanation: Words ending in CIOUS or TIOUS: delicious, cautious, spacious.

title: AL ending
shortDescription: Many adjectives end in AL: natural, national, personal.
fullExplanation: Many adjectives end in AL: natural, national, personal.

title: EL ending
shortDescription: EL often ends words from other languages: angel, model, tunnel.
fullExplanation: EL often ends words from other languages: angel, model, tunnel.

title: LE ending
shortDescription: LE is the most common ending: table, apple, simple, little.
fullExplanation: LE is the most common ending: table, apple, simple, little.

title: LY ending
shortDescription: LY creates adverbs: quickly, slowly, carefully, happily.
fullExplanation: LY creates adverbs: quickly, slowly, carefully, happily.

title: FUL ending
shortDescription: FUL means full of: beautiful, wonderful, careful, hopeful.
fullExplanation: FUL means full of: beautiful, wonderful, careful, hopeful.

title: CEDE/CEED/SEDE
shortDescription: Only 3 words end in CEED: exceed, proceed, succeed.
fullExplanation: Only 3 words end in CEED: exceed, proceed, succeed.

title: V never ends English words
shortDescription: English words never end in V. Add silent E: have, give, love, live.
fullExplanation: English words never end in V. Add silent E: have, give, love, live.

title: X never doubled
shortDescription: X is never doubled in English. It already says /ks/.
fullExplanation: X is never doubled in English. It already says /ks/.

title: K before N is silent
shortDescription: KN: the K is silent. Knee, knife, knock, know, knight.
fullExplanation: KN: the K is silent. Knee, knife, knock, know, knight.

title: W before R is silent
shortDescription: WR: the W is silent. Write, wrong, wrap, wrist, wreck.
fullExplanation: WR: the W is silent. Write, wrong, wrap, wrist, wreck.

title: FLOSS rule
shortDescription: After a short vowel, F, L, S, Z are doubled: staff, bell, moss, buzz.
fullExplanation: After a short vowel, F, L, S, Z are doubled: staff, bell, moss, buzz.

title: Anglo-Saxon words
shortDescription: Short, common, everyday words: house, water, mother, father, eat, drink.
fullExplanation: Short, common, everyday words: house, water, mother, father, eat, drink.

title: Latin words
shortDescription: Academic and formal words: education, communication, transportation.
fullExplanation: Academic and formal words: education, communication, transportation.

title: Greek words
shortDescription: Science and philosophy: philosophy, psychology, technology, photograph.
fullExplanation: Science and philosophy: philosophy, psychology, technology, photograph.

title: French words
shortDescription: Food, fashion, art: restaurant, ballet, boutique, chauffeur.
fullExplanation: Food, fashion, art: restaurant, ballet, boutique, chauffeur.

title: The Layer Test
shortDescription: Check if a word is Anglo-Saxon (short), Latin (medium), or Greek (long).
fullExplanation: Check if a word is Anglo-Saxon (short), Latin (medium), or Greek (long).

shortDescription: Advanced spelling pattern #61 for expert spellers.
fullExplanation: Advanced spelling pattern #61 for expert spellers.
